title: Salman Rushdie’s narrative style in the short story ‘The Free Radio,’
description:
Few writers weaponise an unreliable narrator quite like Salman Rushdie does in The Free Radio, where a well-meaning but self-deceived rickshaw-puller named Ramani trades his future for a fantasy — and the town gossip telling us the story may be just as blind as he is. This short story from East West is a masterclass in dramatic irony and narrative ambiguity, making Rushdie's narrative style the real subject worth studying for BA English Honours and DU SOL students. The gap between what the narrator believes and what the reader sees is where the story's darkest, sharpest meaning lives.
